nodes.name: activate-task-before-work
nodes.tags: task, set_active, activate, workflow, task-framework, context, checkpoint
nodes.body:
Always call tasks__set_active before touching any file or reading code for a task — not after.

**Why:** Activating a task does two things: (1) it transitions status open→active so tasks__finish works cleanly at the end without DB workarounds, and (2) the LangGraph checkpoint starts injecting task context (body, decisions, history, related tasks, related commits) into every subsequent turn. That context makes the work better — Claude sees the plan, prior decisions, and related code chunks automatically. Skipping activation means working blind and then hitting gate errors at close time.

**How to apply:** The moment a task is identified for the current session, call tasks__set_active first — before reading files, before planning, before any edits. For multi-subtask work, activate each subtask in turn as you move to it.

nodes.name: always-activate-task-after-create
nodes.tags: task, create, activate, set_active, context, memory, task-events, workflow,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Always call tasks__set_active immediately after tasks__create, in the same response.

**Why:** Memory injection into task_events (task-memory logging) only works when a task is active. Creating without activating means the first turns of work go untracked — no memory IDs, no turn history for that session.

**How to apply:** Any time tasks__create is called, follow it with tasks__set_active(task_id=<new_id>, session_id=<current_session_id>) before doing any other work.

nodes.name: checkpoint-is-ipc
nodes.tags: langgraph,checkpoint,sqlitesaver,sessions-db,prompt_id,ipc,sessionstate,hooks,cross-process,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
SessionState flows between hooks via SqliteSaver checkpoint (keyed by session_id/thread_id), not by re-reading sessions.db.

Rules:
- `run_session` (UPS) is the full state initializer. `_fresh_state` is a shared fallback also used by `run_gate` (PreToolUse) when no UPS checkpoint exists yet for the session.
- prompt_id, domains, keywords, memories are all in the checkpoint — gate_check and log_tool_usage read them from there, never from DB.
- set_prompt_id writes UUID to checkpoint only (no DB write mid-turn). sessions.db is written once at Stop via run_stop.
- pre_tool_use / run_gate is always fail-open — it never exits 2, even in dev mode.

**Why:** LangServe was stateless (each invoke discarded SessionState). SqliteSaver is cross-process safe and makes the graph the session store.
**How to apply:** When adding new hook logic that needs prior-turn data, read from state (it's in the checkpoint). Never add a DB read in gate_check or log_tool_usage. If no UPS checkpoint exists for a session, run_gate generates a fallback prompt_id (prefixed "fb-").

nodes.name: classify-domain-node-design
nodes.tags: classify-domain, domain-classifier, langgraph, node, weighted-scoring, json-config, icloud,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
ClassifyDomainNode (langchain_learning/nodes/classify_domain.py) loads domain_classifier.json from iCloud (src.config.domain_classifier_json) and uses full weighted scoring: strong/weak keyword signals, combination bigrams/trigrams, negative signals, and CWD map. Result feeds domains + skip_tools back into SessionState. Matched keywords from scoring are merged into state["keywords"] to enrich downstream BM25 in ScoreToolsNode. Score logging at INFO level shows top matched signals per domain. CWD signal comes from SessionState["cwd"] (threaded from hook input), not os.getcwd(). _DC_CACHE is module-level for one-load-per-process; invalidation via mtime check is a known TODO.

Why: Flat vocab set (_DOMAIN_VOCAB) was too coarse — no weighting, no phrases, no negative signals. The JSON config (externalized, iCloud-synced) is the authoritative source.
How to apply: When editing ClassifyDomainNode or domain_classifier.json, remember the CWD field must be in SessionState and passed from hook input. Keywords enrichment feeds score_tools — changes here affect tool hint retrieval.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-code-rag-scope-decision
nodes.tags: code-rag, rag, embeddings, python, docs, full-repo, ast, chunking, scope, design-decision, index
nodes.body:
Index the full repo (Python source + docs/) in code_rag — not docs-only.

**Why:** Empirically tested June 2024 — compared docs-only vs full-repo against 10 real task titles from the task DB. Full-repo won 7/10 by returning actual source locations (e.g., `load_memories.py:_bm25_fallback`) instead of doc sections. Implementation-oriented queries benefit most; the source chunk tells you *where to edit*, a doc section just tells you *what it does*.

**How to apply:**
- `build_code_embeddings.py` chunks `.py` files by AST top-level function/class and `.md` files in `docs/` by `##` section
- SKIP_DIRS excludes `.venv`, `__pycache__`, `.git`, `node_modules`, `tests`
- `/gc` skill re-indexes changed `.py` and `.md` files after every commit
- Do not revert to docs-only — the comparison data justifies the extra index size
- If adding a new RAG scope (e.g. configs, scripts), default to including it rather than excluding

nodes.name: claude-hooks-commit-gate
nodes.tags: claude-hooks,commit,gate,task-id,message
nodes.body:
GitCommitGate (hooks/gates.py) blocks any Bash tool call containing `git commit` unless a `task:<id>` pattern appears anywhere in the command string (commit message body). Blocks with: 'Blocked: git commit is missing a task:<id> reference.' GitCommitMcpGate covers the MCP git__commit variant.

**Why:** Enforces traceability — every commit must reference an active task.
**How to apply:** Always pass task:<id> in the commit message body, or activate a task first with tasks__set_active.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-constraints
nodes.tags: constraints, fail-open, fast, pre_tool_use, exit code, degradation, prod mode, performance
nodes.body:
Hooks must be fast and fail-open.

**How to apply:**
- `pre_tool_use` must never exit 2 (block) on error — always degrade gracefully
- All hooks degrade silently in prod mode if the server is unreachable
- No hook path should block Claude on a slow DB query, network call, or exception
- New nodes must be benchmarked — UPS target is under 300ms end-to-end

nodes.name: claude-hooks-current-gates
nodes.tags: gates, current gates, imessage, mail, git commit, task lifecycle, IMessageSendGate, MailComposeGate, MailDeleteGate, GitCommitGate, TaskSetActiveGate, TaskUpdateGate, TaskFinishGate, JiraHierarchyGate
nodes.body:
Current gates (hooks/gates.py) as of June 2026 (review system removed):
- IMessageSendGate: contacts__search within 120s + searched name must appear in prompt
- MailComposeGate: same as above
- MailDeleteGate: mail__read within 120s
- GitCommitGate (Bash): git commit command must contain task:<id> pattern
- GitCommitMcpGate (git__commit): task_id param must be non-empty
- JiraHierarchyGate (tasks__create): story/task/bug must have epic parent; subtask must have story/task/bug parent
- TaskSetActiveGate (tasks__set_active): enforces open/blocked→active via _check_task_transition
- TaskUpdateGate (tasks__update): all status changes go through _check_task_transition
- TaskFinishGate (tasks__finish): enforces active→done via _check_task_transition

_check_task_transition(task_id, to_status, body) is the single shared helper — all lifecycle gates call it. No manual approval bypass, no review check.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-databases
nodes.tags: databases, SQLite, paths, MEMORY.sqlite, proj_tasks, checkpoints, server_memory, tool_hints, claude_hooks, iCloud
nodes.body:
All SQLite databases used by claude-hooks:

- ~/.claude/MEMORY.sqlite                                                        — long-term memories
- ~/.claude/proj_tasks.db                                                        — task store
- ~/.claude/langgraph_checkpoints.db                                             — SqliteSaver checkpoint store
- ~/.claude/server_memory.sqlite                                                 — ServerMemory durable store (rolling 1000-event window)
- ~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/Databases/tool_hints.sqlite    — MCP tool usage hints (iCloud)
- ~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/Databases/claude_hooks.sqlite  — hook logs (iCloud)

nodes.name: claude-hooks-db-ownership
nodes.tags: claude-hooks, db, database, ownership, status, transitions
nodes.body:
DB ownership (as of June 2026): claude_for_mac_local has NO access to any claude-hooks DB.

All tasks__*, memory__*, hooks__*, session__* tools → mcp__claude-hooks__* only.
session_id → always read from ## Turn state block. Use hooks__checkpoint_query for programmatic access.

Task status transitions: open → active → done (or abandoned/blocked).
No review state — it was removed June 2026. tasks__finish requires active→done directly.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-deploy-workflow
nodes.tags: deploy deployment workflow ship test main worktree pipeline skill launchctl kickstart
nodes.body:
Deployment is fully automated via /deploy skill — no need to know server details or run deploy.sh manually.

Workflow:
1. Edit in dev worktree (`~/workspace/claude-hooks-dev`)
2. Unit tests: `uv run python -m pytest tests/ -q -m 'not integration'`
3. Commit: `/gc`
4. Ship: `/deploy` — runs unit gate in dev, merges to test, restarts server via `launchctl kickstart -k`, runs full suite (unit + integration), then ships to main

Server log after restart: /tmp/claude-hooks-server.log

**Why:** Streamlined in June 2026 — /deploy skill wraps deploy.sh so no server knowledge required.
**How to apply:** Always use /deploy instead of running deploy.sh commands directly. To apply plist edits (not just a restart), run bootout + bootstrap instead — see memory: launchd-plist-reload-required.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-docs-in-repo
nodes.tags: docs,rag,repo,canonical,architecture,setup,onboarding,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Keep all canonical docs for claude-hooks inside the repo under docs/ — not the vault.

**Why:** The repo has a code RAG (code_rag__index_files). Docs committed here are indexed and searchable, making them injectable into context via /explain and smart_search. Vault docs are not indexed by the code RAG.

**How to apply:** Any time writing architecture docs, setup guides, onboarding docs, or decision records for this project — write to docs/ in the repo. After committing, index with mcp__local-mac__code_rag__index_files. Vault is for personal notes and cross-project references only.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-domain-detection
nodes.tags: domain, cwd, detection, cwd_domain_detect, config, domain map, project domain, classify
nodes.body: Domain is detected deterministically — no ML, no scoring. cwd_domain_detect matches state["cwd"] against CWD_DOMAIN_MAP in src/config.py (substring match, first key wins). Valid domains declared in VALID_DOMAINS. Map reloaded fresh on every hook invocation so edits take effect without server restart. load_memories and score_tools infer domain directly from cwd (not from state["domains"]) because they run in parallel with cwd_domain_detect.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-gate-framework
nodes.tags: gates, PreToolUse, gate check, deny, allow, anti-hallucination, Gate ABC, prereq decorator, GATES registry, GateContext, add gate
nodes.body: Gate framework: Gate subclasses in hooks/gates.py, dispatched from GateCheckNode via gates.check(tool_name, ctx). verify() returns (deny, reason). Adding a gate: subclass Gate, set tool_name, implement verify() or use @prereq(tool, window_s, name_arg) decorator, register in GATES dict. GateContext carries prompt_tools, session_tools, prompt_text, and helpers ctx.called_recently(tool, window_s). Fail-open by default; add tool to _FAIL_CLOSED_TOOLS in dispatcher.py for irreversible tools.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-gate-prereq-tracking
nodes.tags: gates, prereq, prompt_tools, session_tools, tool name, MCP prefix, strip, checkpoint, gate context, called_recently
nodes.body: Gate prereq checks read prompt_tools and session_tools from the LangGraph checkpoint. Tool names are MCP-prefix-stripped in log_tool_usage so both gate and log see the same short name (e.g. "contacts__search" not "mcp__local-mac__contacts__search"). ctx.called_recently(tool, window_s) searches both current prompt and session history within the time window. This is the only record of which tools ran this prompt — checkpoint is authoritative.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-graph-event-routing
nodes.tags: session graph, event routing, user_prompt_submit, pre_tool_use, post_tool_use, stop, LangGraph, nodes
nodes.body:
Event routing in session_graph.py (one unified StateGraph for all 4 hook events):

- user_prompt_submit → load_turn → (task active?) → load_active_task → [tier-1 parallel] → [tier-2 parallel] → set_prompt_id → log_task_events → END
- pre_tool_use       → gate_check → END
- post_tool_use      → log_tool_usage → (conditional bridge) → END
- stop               → noop → END

Key files: langchain_learning/session_graph.py (wiring), langchain_learning/nodes/registry.py (NODE_REGISTRY).

nodes.name: claude-hooks-hook-delivery
nodes.tags: hook delivery, client.py, curl, FastAPI, dispatcher, LangGraph, pipeline, entry point
nodes.body:
How a Claude Code hook event reaches the graph:

Claude Code fires hook → hooks/client.py <Event>
  → enriches payload with CLAUDE_CWD
  → curl POST http://127.0.0.1:8766/hook/<Event>
  → FastAPI server (hooks/server.py, persistent, launchd-managed)
  → hooks/dispatcher.py _handle_<event>()
  → LangGraph session graph (SqliteSaver, in-process)

Checkpoint DB: ~/.claude/langgraph_checkpoints.db (persists across restarts)
Fail-open: client.py exits 0 with {} if server unreachable.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-hook-payload-shape
nodes.tags: claude-hooks,hook,payload,mcp,tool_name,tool_response,normalize
nodes.body:
Hook payloads from Claude Code arrive as JSON on stdin to client.py. client.py enriches with CLAUDE_CWD env var and POSTs to the server. PreToolUse payloads have `tool_name` (full MCP name like `mcp__local-mac__imessage__send`) and `tool_input`. PostToolUse adds `tool_response`. dispatcher.py strips the `mcp__<server>__` prefix via `strip_mcp_prefix()` for gate matching.

**How to apply:** Use `hook_input.get('tool_name', '')` in dispatcher; gates match on short names (e.g. 'imessage__send', not full MCP name).

nodes.name: claude-hooks-hybrid-model-strategy
nodes.tags: ollama,claude,hybrid,classification,accuracy,qwen,langchain-ollama,design,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Goal: use Claude + Ollama (qwen2.5:3b) as a hybrid — each at its optimal strength without compromising accuracy.

Rule: Ollama for fast/cheap/local tasks (domain classification, signal extraction, lightweight generation). Claude for reasoning, gating decisions, and anything where a wrong answer has real consequences. Never use a local model for safety-critical decisions (gates, send/delete operations).

Why: zero API cost and low latency for classification-scale tasks; Claude's accuracy preserved where it matters.

How to apply: when adding a new pipeline node, ask — does this require judgment or is it pattern-matching/extraction? Pattern-matching → Ollama candidate. Judgment/safety → Claude only.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-jinja2-ui-reference
nodes.tags: jinja2, templates, UI, frontend, macros, icons, template inheritance, filters, HTML, ui development, globals, urls, environment
nodes.body:
Jinja2 patterns and best practices for the claude-hooks UI are documented in docs/jinja2.md.

Covers: template inheritance (base.html + block slots), includes, macros (icons centralized in partials/icons.html), namespace for loop variables, filters (|length, |safe, |split), inline for-loop filtering, the `or` default pattern, conditional CSS classes, and environment globals.

Environment globals (added 2026-06-26): JINJA_ENV.globals['urls'] in hooks/ui/deps.py holds all /ui/* route paths. Every template uses {{ urls.tasks }}, {{ urls.memory }}, etc. — no hardcoded strings. Dynamic segments are concatenated: {{ urls.tasks }}{{ task.id }}. A dict was chosen over url_for() because base paths are static strings with no parameters.

All examples are pulled from hooks/templates/ui/ — each section shows exactly which file uses the pattern.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-key-files
nodes.tags: key files, file map, client.py, server.py, dispatcher.py, gates.py, session_graph.py, registry.py, deploy.sh, paths.py
nodes.body:
Key files in the claude-hooks repo:

- hooks/client.sh                             — thin curl wrapper, enriches payload with CLAUDE_CWD, fail-open
- hooks/server.py                             — FastAPI server, SqliteSaver lifecycle, /health + /session endpoints
- hooks/dispatcher.py                         — all 4 hook event handlers + task body section gate
- hooks/gates.py                              — gate framework + all Gate subclasses + _check_task_transition helper
- langchain_learning/session_graph.py         — LangGraph graph wiring (all 4 events)
- langchain_learning/nodes/registry.py        — NODE_REGISTRY, one class per node
- src/tools/tasks.py                          — all tasks MCP tool handlers
- src/tools/memory.py                         — all memory MCP tool handlers
- scripts/deploy.sh                           — dev→test (unit gate + full suite + reload); --ship for test→main

nodes.name: claude-hooks-load-memories-node
nodes.tags: load_memories, LoadMemoriesNode, combination signal, tag overlap, BM25, domain weight, recency, memory retrieval, injection, scoring, MEMORY.sqlite
nodes.body:
LoadMemoriesNode retrieves top-5 memories via combination signal scoring (no embeddings, no external services):

Scoring per row:
  1. Domain weight   — project_domain=2.0 | global=0.5 | other=skip (0)
  2. Tag overlap     — Jaccard(prompt_tokens ∩ tag_tokens) × 3.0  (primary signal, hand-authored tags)
  3. Body overlap    — Jaccard(prompt_tokens ∩ body_tokens) × 1.0
  4. Recency boost   — ×1.2 if updated ≤30d, ×0.8 if ≥180d

All rows scanned (up to 500) in a single SQL read; scored in Python; top-5 returned.

Key behaviors:
- Global memories NOT auto-included — must earn a slot via keyword overlap (score 0.5 + overlap)
- Out-of-domain memories (neither project nor global) skip entirely — score=0
- No Ollama, no TurboVec, no iCloud read. Pure SQLite.

Tuning: when a wrong memory surfaces (visible in sqlite logs), tighten its tags. When a relevant memory is missed, add the right keyword to its tags. Tags are the primary retrieval lever.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-load-vault-context
nodes.tags: load_vault_context, LIFE_OS, soul.md, Debaditya.md, memory.md, always-on identity layer, soul context injection, vault files
nodes.body:
`_load_vault_context()` in `hooks/dispatcher.py` reads three LIFE_OS md files on every UserPromptSubmit turn and injects them as `## Soul context` in `additionalSystemPrompt`.

Files read (direct filesystem, no MCP):
- `~/workspace/claude_documents/LIFE_OS/soul.md` → "### Soul"
- `~/workspace/claude_documents/LIFE_OS/Debaditya.md` → "### Identity"
- `~/workspace/claude_documents/LIFE_OS/memory.md` → "### Memory"

**Why:** Hermes-style always-on identity layer — complements BM25-scored SQLite memories with human-editable vault files. Always injected regardless of active task. Missing files are silently skipped (no caching, no complexity).

**How to apply:** Edit the three md files in Obsidian to update identity/soul/memory context. Do not add caching or conditional skipping logic — intentionally simple. Append durable notes via `vault__append` to `LIFE_OS/memory.md`.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-mcp-hooks-boundary
nodes.tags: MCP, hooks, boundary, DB ownership, checkpoint, proj_tasks, MEMORY.sqlite, write rule, architecture
nodes.body:
Hard DB ownership boundary between MCP tools and hook nodes:

- MCP tools  → write to their own domain DBs only (proj_tasks.db, MEMORY.sqlite). NEVER write to LangGraph checkpoint.
- Hook nodes → own the LangGraph checkpoint (SqliteSaver). React to tool calls via PostToolUse bridge nodes.
- hooks__checkpoint_query is read-only from MCP — the one permitted exception.

Doc: docs/arch/mcp_hooks_boundary.md

nodes.name: claude-hooks-mission
nodes.tags: mission, goal, purpose, context, long-running tasks, sessions, memory injection, tool hints, session tracking
nodes.body:
**Core goal: maintain context across long-running tasks** — Claude should never lose track of where a multi-session task stands, what decisions were made, and what's left. This is the reason the project exists.

claude-hooks is a LangGraph-based context injection system for Claude Code. It intercepts all four hook events (UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop) to build a persistent memory + session tracking layer around Claude.

**Mission:** make Claude context-aware across sessions — injecting relevant memories, tool hints, and session summaries automatically, without user intervention.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-node-design
nodes.tags: node, design, callable class, SessionState, partial dict, timing, wrap, NODE_REGISTRY, observability, phase, parallel, sequential
nodes.body: Each node is a callable class (class FooNode: def __call__(self, state) -> dict). Nodes read only from state, return only fields they modify. Timing instrumentation (→ node_name, ← node_name Xms) applied at graph build time via wrap(name, fn) — not inside node files. NODE_REGISTRY + get_node() factory keeps build_session_graph() as pure wiring. Phase (parallel/sequential) logged via _PARALLEL_NODES frozenset in langchain_learning/nodes/_node_log.py — update this frozenset when graph topology changes.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-observability-principle
nodes.tags: observability, logging, debug, logs, sqlite, claude_hooks.sqlite, INFO, node, MCP tool, timing, diagnosis
nodes.body:
Debug from logs first — never from source code.

All hook events (UPS, PTU, PreTU, Stop), MCP tool calls, and node execution must be readable from `claude_hooks.sqlite` alone. If a behavior can't be diagnosed from log rows, that's a logging gap to fix.

**How to apply:**
- Every new node, handler, or MCP tool must emit INFO rows at entry/exit with timing and key state
- When debugging unexpected behavior: read logs first, grep source second
- Logging gaps (missing entry/exit rows) are bugs, not cosmetic issues

nodes.name: claude-hooks-posttooluse-bridge
nodes.tags: PostToolUse, bridge nodes, ActivateTaskNode, DeactivateTaskNode, DecisionTaskNode, checkpoint, task lifecycle, conditional, files backfill
nodes.body:
PostToolUse conditional bridge nodes react to specific MCP tool calls and write to the LangGraph checkpoint:

- tasks__set_active / tasks__pop_active  → ActivateTaskNode   — writes active_task_id + task_memories to checkpoint; also backfills memories.files=NULL for domain-matched memories using the task's Files: section (up to 10 per activation, stem-token matching)
- tasks__clear_active / tasks__finish    → DeactivateTaskNode — zeros active task in checkpoint
- tasks__add_decision                    → DecisionTaskNode   — appends to mid_task_decisions in checkpoint

Rule: MCP tools never write to checkpoint directly — bridge nodes are the only path.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-readme-style
nodes.tags: readme, pitch-writing, conversational, style, new-users, documentation
nodes.body:
When writing about the task framework in the claude-hooks README, use a conversational pitch style — not technical docs, not formatted flow tables.

**Why:** The README is for new users who need to understand the value, not the internals. A conversational tone makes it feel approachable and human, while still showing the end-to-end flow.

**How to apply:** Write as if explaining it to someone who asked "what does this do?" — state the problem plainly, explain what it solves in plain language, then show a minimal inline example of the actual commands. Avoid headers-within-sections, bullet lists of features, or internal implementation details.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-recency-pull
nodes.tags: recency, drift, meta-goal, tension, mission, direction, distraction, session review
nodes.body:
The system will always be drawn toward the latest task, freshest session, most recent signal. Resist this.

**What recency pull looks like — recognize and resist:**
- Refining scoring pipeline indefinitely instead of advancing tools.db or task ancestry
- Cleaning up memories or CLAUDE.md instead of advancing tool saturation (task:4d50496b)
- Adding observability polish beyond boundary correctness
- Working on the most recent bug without checking if it serves the mission
- Any session where no open task from the backlog was advanced

**The test:** at the end of a session, did the work move the mission forward, or did it just service the present moment?

When in doubt, re-read [[claude-hooks-mission]] and [[claude-hooks-open-work]] before starting a new task.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-related-tasks-node
nodes.tags: related tasks, load_related_tasks, neighbors, TurboVec, embeddings, tasks_embeddings, Ollama, nomic-embed-text, semantic search, done tasks
nodes.body: load_related_tasks embeds active task title+body via Ollama (nomic-embed-text) and queries .tasks_embeddings.tvim (TurboVec) for top semantically similar tasks filtered to status=done, capped at 3. Index rebuilt automatically on MCP server startup if missing; incrementally upserted on tasks__create, tasks__finish, tasks__set_active. Empty results are normal for novel tasks with no prior done neighbours. Injected as "## Related past tasks" in system prompt.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-reliable-hook-placement
nodes.tags: hooks reliability UPS UserPromptSubmit SessionStart SessionEnd placement critical
nodes.body:
UserPromptSubmit (UPS) is the only reliable hook for critical logic in claude-hooks.

**Why:** SessionStart and SessionEnd are unreliable — not guaranteed to fire on crashes, force-quits, reconnects, or abnormal exits. UPS fires on every user turn, is synchronous, and blocks until it returns.

**How to apply:** Any logic that must run consistently (e.g. checkpoint trimming, session cap enforcement) belongs in the UPS handler, not SessionStart/SessionEnd.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-replay-harness
nodes.tags: replay, harness, baseline, regression, capture, test, main, graph stability, replay_baseline.json, claude_hooks.sqlite
nodes.body:
Replay harness detects graph regressions against real production UPS events.

Workflow:
1. Capture baseline from **main worktree** (`~/workspace/claude-hooks`) — main is the known-good state.
2. After changes land on test (`deploy.sh`), run `--replay` from **test worktree** to diff against the main baseline.
3. If all good, `deploy.sh --ship` promotes test→main and update the baseline.

Diffs scored on: domains, memories_count, tools_count, related, rag_chunks.
Baseline lives at tests/replay_baseline.json (committed to the repo).

**Why capture from main:** main is the stable validated state — baseline must represent known-good behavior, not in-flight dev changes.

**If baseline was captured with broken code:** regenerate from main after the fix is shipped.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-review-system
nodes.tags: review, removed, task lifecycle, history
nodes.body:
The review system (review state, review templates, LoadActiveReviewNode, execute_review, submit_review_item MCP tools) was REMOVED in June 2026 (commit 4a1e714).

Task lifecycle is now: open → active → done (or abandoned/blocked). No review state exists.

**Why removed:** Review gate was a friction point with no adoption — tasks were never actually reviewed through the system. Replaced by /task-introspection skill (post-task retrospective on demand).

**How to apply:** Do not reference review state, review templates, or review MCP tools — they no longer exist.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-server-deploy-restart
nodes.tags: deploy restart launchctl kickstart server reload worktree dev test main pipeline
nodes.body: Server has no --reload. Restart is always via deploy.sh after merging dev→test: `launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u)/<plist-label>`. Never restart manually. Use /deploy skill which runs deploy.sh (dev→test, unit gate + full suite) then deploy.sh --ship (test→main). Deploy is the only path to restart.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-server-identity
nodes.tags: server-session-id, fastapi, health-endpoint, server-memory, sqlite, cross-session, server-restart
nodes.body: The hook server generates a unique SERVER_SESSION_ID (UUID) on startup, stored in server_memory.py. Logged at startup: `hook-server: started, server_session=<id>`. Exposed via GET /health which returns {status: 'ok'}. The server_session_id distinguishes the running server instance — useful for detecting restarts. Session data is stored in SqliteSaver checkpoints keyed by claude session_id, not server_session_id.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-server-process
nodes.tags: server FastAPI persistent launchd port 8766 hook server plist install health check server log
nodes.body: Hook server is a persistent FastAPI process (hooks/server.py) on port 8766, managed by launchd. Plist generated at install time from launchd/com.claude-hooks-pipeline.plist.template — install_server.sh substitutes __HOME__, __UV__, __UV_DIR__, __REPO__ with real values. Runs from the test worktree (~~/workspace/claude-hooks-test). Health check: curl http://127.0.0.1:8766/health → {"status":"ok"}. Server log: /tmp/claude-hooks-server.log.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-session-lifecycle
nodes.tags: session, lifecycle, checkpoint, SqliteSaver, evict, session_id, langgraph_checkpoints, UPS, trim, cap
nodes.body:
Session state is stored in SqliteSaver at ~/.claude/langgraph_checkpoints.db keyed by session_id. State survives server restarts.

Checkpoint cap: `_trim_checkpoints()` runs on every UserPromptSubmit — keeps the 2 most recently active sessions (`_CHECKPOINT_SESSION_CAP=2`), evicts the rest. This ensures stale sessions never accumulate regardless of server uptime.

Stop fires every turn and must never evict checkpoints — doing so would wipe cross-turn state like prompt_tools and session_tools needed by gates.

**Why:** SessionEnd is unreliable (not guaranteed on crash/force-quit). UPS is the only hook that fires consistently every turn, so checkpoint hygiene lives there.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-skill-mcp-pattern
nodes.tags: architecture,skills,mcp,pattern,layering,api,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Skills are the UI layer; MCP tools are the API layer; databases are storage. Each skill action maps to one or more MCP tool calls — analogous to a UI action calling multiple API endpoints in full-stack dev.

Example: /pause (skill/UI) → tasks__pause (MCP tool/API) → proj_tasks.db (storage)

**Why:** keeps skills thin (behavior contracts only) and MCP tools reusable, testable, and semantically named in logs. Raw tasks__update with manual markdown in a skill is a smell — if a skill repeats a formatting pattern, extract it into a dedicated MCP tool.

**How to apply:** when adding a new skill action that writes structured data, ask whether the write deserves its own MCP tool with a clean signature rather than a generic tasks__update/memory__add call.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-stop-vs-sessionend
nodes.tags: claude-hooks, stop, sessionend, lifecycle, per-turn, UPS, checkpoint, trim, evict, reliable
nodes.body:
Stop hook fires at the end of EVERY assistant response — not at session end. Never do session-scoped cleanup on Stop — it wipes cross-turn state every turn.

SessionEnd and SessionStart are unreliable — not guaranteed to fire on crashes, force-quits, or reconnects. Do NOT rely on them for critical logic.

**Correct wiring:**
- Stop → finalise turn only (no eviction)
- SessionEnd → unreliable, do not depend on it
- UPS → checkpoint trim runs here (2-session cap enforced on every prompt)

**Why:** Checkpoint hygiene was moved from SessionEnd to UPS precisely because SessionEnd cannot be trusted to always fire.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-task-body-gate
nodes.tags: task, gate, dispatcher, scaffold, sections, reject, create
nodes.body:
task body MUST start with `Type: <kind>` and contain that kind's required sections — else tasks__create rejects it.

**Why:** Gate is enforced in hooks/dispatcher.py _TASK_BODY_SECTIONS.

**How to apply:** Copy scaffold from repo `task_templates/<kind>.md`. Kinds and required sections:
- feature → Task, Motivation, Resolution, Files
- bug → Task, Cause, Resolution, Files
- research → Task, Finding, Context, Files
- misc → Task, Resolution, Notes, Files
- epic → use tasks__create_epic(title, motivation) — builds body internally, skips the gate

nodes.name: claude-hooks-task-state-machine
nodes.tags: task, state machine, status, transitions, open, active, done, abandoned, blocked, handle_update, is_valid_transition
nodes.body:
Task status state machine (as of June 2026, review state removed):

open → active → done
active → open        (requeue)
blocked → active     (unblock)
any state → abandoned

handle_update() in src/tools/tasks.py is the ONLY path for status transitions — it calls is_valid_transition() to enforce the machine.
Nodes must NEVER run `UPDATE open_tasks SET status=...` directly — bypasses the guard and breaks gate enforcement.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-test-isolation
nodes.tags: claude-hooks,test,isolation,prefix,guard,prod
nodes.body:
Test sessions are identified by session_id prefix: `_TEST_PREFIXES = ('test-', 'pytest-', 'api-test-')` in hooks/server_memory.py. Any session whose claude_session_id starts with these prefixes is treated as a test session and guarded from writing to prod state. Integration tests use pytest fixtures that inject test-prefixed session IDs to avoid polluting prod DBs.

**How to apply:** Never use a real session ID in integration tests — always use a test-prefixed ID.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-tool-tracking
nodes.tags: PostToolUse, log_tool_usage, tool hints, tool tracking, tool_hints.sqlite, upsert, latency, keywords, prompt_tools, session_tools, ThreadPoolExecutor, task_events
nodes.body:
PostToolUse runs log_tool_usage node which does 3 things concurrently via ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2):
1. Upserts tool_hints.sqlite — increments count, updates rolling avg latency, appends prompt to recent_prompts (last 10 kept), seeds keywords from tool name tokens + domain if empty
2. Appends tool name to task_events.tools for current prompt_id in proj_tasks.db
3. Updates prompt_tools and session_tools in LangGraph checkpoint

Gate prereq checks read prompt_tools/session_tools from checkpoint — tool names are MCP-prefix-stripped so both gate and log see the same short name.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-type-vocabulary
nodes.tags: claude-hooks, tasks, issue_type, type, vocabulary
nodes.body:
Two DIFFERENT type vocabularies in the task system — easy to confuse:

- body `Type:` ∈ {feature, bug, research, misc, epic} — workflow kind, controls required body sections
- `issue_type` param ∈ {bug, epic, story, subtask, task} — DB hierarchy (review was removed June 2026)

`issue_type='task'` REQUIRES a parent epic — pass parent_id=<epic_id>. Prefer `issue_type='story'` for child tasks under an epic.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-ups-pipeline
nodes.tags: pipeline, graph, UPS, UserPromptSubmit, langgraph, nodes, fan-out, parallel, session graph, topology, active task, task context, task context creation, context injection, tier1, tier2, how task context works
nodes.body:
When a task is active, UPS builds task context by running 4 nodes in parallel (tier 1): load_task_history, load_task_code, load_related_tasks, load_related_commits. (load_active_review was removed June 2026 when the review system was removed.)

Two parallel fan-out tiers:
Tier 1 (task context nodes): load_active_task → [load_task_history ∥ load_task_code ∥ load_related_tasks ∥ load_related_commits]
Tier 2 (context nodes): fan-in → [cwd_domain_detect ∥ load_memories ∥ score_tools] → set_prompt_id → log_task_events → END
Without an active task, all tier-1 nodes return empty dicts and tier-2 runs context-only.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-wiki-pattern
nodes.tags: docs,wiki,hub-and-spoke,nav,architecture,reference,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Wiki structure for claude-hooks docs established in task:80736605 (2026-06-12).

Pattern: hub-and-spoke with nav footer on every spoke page:
  ← [Architecture](../ARCHITECTURE.md) · [RelatedA](relatedA.md) · [RelatedB](relatedB.md)

Hub: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md — links to all spokes, no nav footer needed.

Spoke nav map:
- graph_pipeline.md  → state.md, system_prompt.md
- state.md           → graph_pipeline.md, task_framework.md
- system_prompt.md   → graph_pipeline.md, task_framework.md
- task_framework.md  → system_prompt.md, databases.md
- databases.md       → task_framework.md, graph_pipeline.md
- design_decisions.md → graph_pipeline.md, mid_task_decisions.md
- mid_task_decisions.md → task_framework.md, design_decisions.md
- setup.md           → new_repo_onboarding.md, databases.md
- new_repo_onboarding.md → setup.md, databases.md
- skills.md          → task_framework.md, new_repo_onboarding.md

Rules when adding a new doc page:
1. Pick the two most naturally related existing pages as "related" links
2. Add this page as a "related" link on at least one of those pages
3. Add it to ARCHITECTURE.md Sections list

Lint note: ordered list steps containing fenced code blocks cause MD029/MD031 cycle — use bold step labels (**1.** **2.**) instead of actual list items.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-worktree-dev
nodes.tags: worktree, git, dev, test, main, branch, deploy, server, sdlc, claude-hooks, launchctl
nodes.body:
claude-hooks uses three git worktrees for a proper SDLC:

- `~/workspace/claude-hooks-dev` → `dev` branch — all edits, quick unit tests, `/gc` commits
- `~/workspace/claude-hooks-test` → `test` branch — server runs here (port 8766, no --reload); full test suite runs against this
- `~/workspace/claude-hooks` → `main` branch — sacred, only receives clean merges via `/deploy`

**Deploy flow:** Use `/deploy` skill — runs unit gate in dev, merges dev→test, restarts the server via `launchctl kickstart -k`, runs full suite, then ships test→main.

**Why:** Server runs from test worktree so dev edits never disrupt live Claude Code hooks. Main is never touched directly.

**How to apply:** All edits go in dev. Never edit test or main directly. Use `/deploy` to ship.

nodes.name: claude-hooks-worktree-test
nodes.tags: worktree, test, server, launchd, port 8766, integration, sdlc, claude-hooks, launchctl, kickstart
nodes.body:
The claude-hooks test worktree at `~/workspace/claude-hooks-test` (test branch) is where the hook server runs.

Launchd plist WorkingDirectory: `~/workspace/claude-hooks-test`. Server runs without --reload on port 8766. deploy.sh restarts it via `launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u)/<plist-label>` after merging dev→test, then health-checks before running the full suite.

Integration and UI tests (pytest -m integration) hit this server via HTTP — they work from any worktree.

**Why:** Isolates the live server from in-progress dev edits. Main is never the server source.

**How to apply:** After /deploy merges dev→test and restarts the server, integration tests run against fresh code. Never point the plist at dev or main. Check /tmp/claude-hooks-server.log if server fails to come up.

**Note:** `launchctl kickstart -k` restarts the process but uses launchd's in-memory plist config. To apply plist edits, run bootout + bootstrap (see memory: launchd-plist-reload-required).

nodes.name: dev-gate-removal-task-close-gotcha
nodes.tags: gate, test server, task close, MCP, review, deploy, worktree, direct DB
nodes.body:
When removing a gate from dev, MCP tool calls that pass through the running test server will still be blocked by the OLD gate until /deploy is run.

**Why:** The test server (claude-hooks-test worktree, port 8766) runs the code from the test branch. Dev edits don't affect it until merged via deploy.sh. So if you remove a gate in dev and then try to call an MCP tool that the old gate blocks, it will fail — even though the new code no longer has the gate.

**How to apply:** After removing a gate in dev, don't try to use MCP tools that the removed gate was blocking (e.g. tasks__finish for tasks stuck in "open" due to old review gate). Either: (a) write directly to the DB as a workaround, or (b) /deploy first so the test server picks up the new gate-free code. Flag this clearly rather than debugging the MCP error as if it's a code bug.

nodes.name: docstrings-improve-rag-chunks
nodes.tags: docstring,rag,code-rag,retrieval,chunks,server,endpoints,functions,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Add meaningful docstrings to all high-level functions, endpoints, and classes so code RAG retrieval surfaces the right chunks for task-related queries.

**Why:** The RAG chunker splits by function/section. Bare functions (no docstring) produce chunks with no semantic signal beyond the name — they won't surface for task queries even when they're the most relevant file. Adding docstrings with key domain terms (e.g. `gate_check`, `permissionDecision`, `name_arg_check`, `claude_hooks.sqlite`) pulls those chunks into top-5 results. Validated: `hooks/server.py` `pre_tool_use` went from absent to #2 (0.748) after docstrings were added.

**How to apply:** When writing or editing any function that owns a meaningful behavior (endpoints, node `__call__`, gate `verify`, dispatcher handlers), add a one-to-three line docstring covering: what it does, what it returns, and any key domain terms (tool names, log fields, DB names) that someone searching for this behavior would use. Especially important for FastAPI endpoints and LangGraph nodes — they're the most-queried entry points.

nodes.name: domain-classifier-llm-when
nodes.tags: domain-classifier haiku use_llm when long-running new-project decision,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Use use_llm=True (Haiku via ChatAnthropic) for domain classification on long-running new projects where semantic understanding matters more than speed.
Why: keyword path misses semantic matches (e.g. "knee pain" → health); Haiku understands intent. Cost (~300ms + API) is acceptable when amortized over a long session.
Use use_llm=False (keyword default) for hooks and short-lived invocations where latency and offline reliability matter.
How to apply: when starting a new project pipeline, consider enabling use_llm=True in build_memory_pipeline(). For the memory_loader hook, always keep keyword default.

nodes.name: exclude-active-session-from-live-index-baseline
nodes.tags: replay_harness, active_session_exclusion, baseline_capture, tool_hints_drift, moving_target_index
nodes.body:
When capturing a regression baseline against a live append-only index, exclude sessions that are still actively generating events.

**Why:** `score_tools` reads from `tool_hints.sqlite`, which grows with every PostToolUse event. The current session's tool count at capture time T differs from what replay sees at T+N seconds — after more tool calls have been logged. All completed sessions have a frozen tool history; only the active session is a moving target. This caused `test_replay_matches_baseline` to fail on every deploy.

**How to apply:** In `replay_harness.py`, `_active_session_ids()` detects sessions whose last UPS event is within 30 minutes and `cmd_capture()` skips them. The general principle: any regression baseline scored against a live index must exclude in-flight sessions — detect them by recency (last event age < threshold), not by an explicit end marker (end markers are unreliable).

nodes.name: feedback-fix-in-dev-first
nodes.tags: deploy, worktree, fix, dev, test, main, workflow, pipeline
nodes.body:
Always fix issues in the dev worktree first, even when the bug is discovered in test or main.

**Why:** Dev is the only place changes should originate — test and main are downstream. Fixing directly in test/main breaks the dev→test→main pipeline and risks divergence.

**How to apply:** When a test failure or bug surfaces during deploy (unit gate, full suite, or post-ship), stop, go to dev, fix there, commit via /gc, then re-run /deploy from the top.

nodes.name: file-token-matching-stem-only
nodes.tags: file tokens stem directory matching memory backfill false positive token overlap slug
nodes.body:
When tokenizing file paths for name/tag overlap matching, use only the filename stem — not directory components. `hooks/gates.py` → {gate, gates}, not {hook, hooks, gate, gates}.

**Why:** Directory names (hooks, src, tests, langchain) appear in nearly every memory slug in the domain, causing false-positive matches. A memory named `claude-hooks-unrelated-foo` would match any file under `hooks/` if directory tokens were included.

**How to apply:** In `_file_tokens()`, split only the last path component (after the final `/`), strip the extension, then split on `_` and `-`. Add naive plural/singular variants. Never call `re.findall` on the full path string.

nodes.name: gate-policy-boundary-in-node
nodes.tags: gate policy protocol GateCheckNode GateContext state transformation boundary separation
nodes.body:
GateCheckNode has two distinct responsibilities — keep them separate:
  1. State transformation: extract tool_name, build GateContext from SessionState (stays in node)
  2. Policy decision: check(tool_name, ctx) → (deny, reason) (delegated to GatePolicy)

The GATES early-return (`if tool_name not in GATES`) also stays in the node — it is routing logic (skip ungated tools), not policy logic.

**Why:** GateContext construction involves ~30 lines of state extraction. It is not part of the policy contract — a different policy impl would still need the same GateContext. Mixing them would make every policy reimplementer rebuild the extraction logic.

**How to apply:** When adding a new Protocol boundary to a node, ask: 'would every alternative implementation need to redo this code?' If yes, it stays in the node. If no, it belongs in the Protocol impl.

nodes.name: gc-skill-task-id-in-commit
nodes.tags: gc,commit,task,task-id,git,cwd,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
When using /gc, always append the active task ID to the commit message body as `task:<id>`.

**Why:** Makes every commit traceable back to the task that drove it. Confirmed working and valued — visible in git log, links code history to task graph.

**How to apply:** Check `## Active task` in system prompt first. If a task ID is present, append `task:<id>` on its own line in the commit body. If `/gc task:<id>` was passed explicitly, use that ID regardless of active task state.

**cwd:** `/Users/debaditya/workspace/claude-hooks`

nodes.name: gitignore-rag-files
nodes.tags: gitignore, rag, embeddings, tvim, claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Always add RAG/embedding index files to .gitignore — never commit them.

**Why:** These are derived artifacts (rebuilt on demand or server startup) and should not be tracked. Files include: `.code_embeddings.tvim`, `.code_embeddings.meta.json`, `.tasks_embeddings.tvim`, `.tasks_embeddings.meta.json`, `.diff_embeddings.tvim`, `.diff_embeddings.meta.json`.

**How to apply:** Whenever creating a new RAG pipeline or embedding index, immediately add its output files to `.gitignore` before any commits.

nodes.name: hard-to-test-means-bad-structure
nodes.tags: testing,refactor,structure,design,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
If a test is getting difficult to write, the code structure is likely the problem — simplify the code, don't force the test.

**Why:** Encountered when patching node `__call__` was awkward due to the `wrap()` decorator in `_node_log.py` sitting between LangGraph and the node. Hard-to-patch code is tightly coupled code.

**How to apply:** When writing a test requires unusual indirection (patching internals, multiple nested mocks, bypassing decorators), stop and ask whether the production code can be restructured to make the dependency injectable or the boundary cleaner. Simplify the code first, then the test becomes obvious.

nodes.name: hook-dev-mode
nodes.tags: claude-hooks, dev-mode, error-handling, exit-2, hook-errors
nodes.body:
Hook error visibility is controlled by LC_DEV_MODE in langchain_learning/config.py (field: dev_mode, default: False).

When LC_DEV_MODE=true: hooks exit with code 2 on any exception — Claude Code surfaces the error inline immediately.
When LC_DEV_MODE=false (stable/prod): hooks write error to log DB silently, no exit 2.

pre_tool_use_lc is always fail-open (no exit 2 regardless of dev_mode) — crashing it would block tool calls.

**Why:** During development, silent failures (like the stale turn=0 kwarg) went unnoticed for an entire session. exit 2 makes breakage immediately visible in the conversation.
**How to apply:** Set LC_DEV_MODE=true in ~/.claude/.env during active development. Remove or set false when hooks are stable.

nodes.name: hooks-paths-module
nodes.tags: paths config project-root vault-root mem-db tool-registry hardcoded paths central module
nodes.body:
All project and user-specific paths for claude-hooks live in `hooks/paths.py`.

Exports:
- `PROJECT_ROOT` — resolves from `__file__`, worktree-safe
- `HOOKS_DIR` — `PROJECT_ROOT / "hooks"`
- `DOCS_DIR` — `PROJECT_ROOT / "docs"`
- `MEM_DB` — `~/.claude/MEMORY.sqlite`
- `VAULT_ROOT` — `~/workspace/claude_documents`
- `TOOL_REGISTRY_PATH` — iCloud tool_registry.json

**Why:** Previously each module computed its own `_PROJECT_ROOT` or hardcoded `Path.home() / 'workspace/claude-hooks'`, causing static files and templates to silently serve from the wrong worktree.

**How to apply:** Any new path constant goes in `hooks/paths.py` — never hardcode paths in other modules. Import with `from hooks.paths import <NAME>`.

nodes.name: invisible-process-principle
nodes.tags: invisible process efficiency incremental backfill automation zero friction design principle
nodes.body:
The most efficient processes are invisible. A bulk one-shot operation (e.g. backfill 89 memories) is expensive and visible — it requires a dedicated task, user attention, and a commit. An incremental operation hooked into normal work (e.g. backfill files=NULL memories during task activation) costs nothing extra and completes naturally over time.

**Why:** Abandoned task:3aeaf2d4 (bulk memory files backfill) in favor of task:512a7587 (auto-backfill in ActivateTaskNode). The activation moment already has the richest context — task files, domain, injected memories — making it the natural place to infer file provenance.

**How to apply:** Before creating a bulk data migration task, ask: is there an existing workflow event where this data is naturally available? If yes, hook into that event instead. Prefer 5 updates per activation over 89 updates once.

nodes.name: langchain-subprocess-runnable
nodes.tags: subprocess,runnable,stdin,stdout,e2e,hook,runnablelambda,external-process,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
LangChain pattern: wrap any stdin→stdout subprocess as a RunnableLambda.
Serialize input dict → JSON → stdin; run subprocess; parse stdout JSON → return dict.
Gives the process .invoke(), .batch(), .stream(), and | pipe support for free.
Used in C6 HookRunnable to e2e-test memory_loader_lc.py the same way Claude Code fires the hook.
Rule: if a program speaks JSON on stdin/stdout, it can be a typed Runnable — same interface as ChatAnthropic.

nodes.name: langchain-vs-claude-code-inversion
nodes.tags: langchain claude-code inversion-of-control architecture mcp lcel programmatic agentic,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Claude Code and LangChain are complementary layers, not alternatives — they represent an inversion of control.
Claude Code: Claude IS the agent. It reasons, picks MCP tools, orchestrates. Python hooks inject context around Claude but cannot compose multi-step LLM chains cleanly. claude -p subprocess = no streaming, hard to compose.
LangChain: Python IS the agent. Claude (ChatAnthropic) is just a Runnable — a typed component with .invoke()/.batch()/.stream(). with_structured_output gives Pydantic-validated responses; RunnableParallel runs LLM calls concurrently; streaming falls out for free.
Stack: Claude Code (agentic reasoning) → MCP tools (native macOS) AND → LangChain pipeline (programmatic, Claude Haiku for narrow sub-tasks with schema contracts).
Rule: use Claude Code when you need reasoning and tool selection. Use LangChain when Python should own the orchestration and Claude is one step in a typed pipeline.

nodes.name: langgraph-checkpointer-pattern
nodes.tags: langgraph, checkpointer, SqliteSaver, thread_id, session_id, cross-hook, stateful-server,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
LangGraph SqliteSaver checkpointer wired into hooks/server.py. session_id maps to thread_id. UserPromptSubmit passes full _fresh_state() + event fields to establish checkpoint. PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop pass only event-specific fields — checkpointer restores the rest. prompt_id, domains, keywords, memories all retained in checkpoint across all four hook events. Zero DB reads between hooks.

Checkpoint DB: ~/.claude/langgraph_checkpoints.db (SqliteSaver, survives server restarts).

Why: LangServe was stateless — each invoke threw away SessionState. SqliteSaver makes the graph the session store and is cross-process safe. Learned from ACME checkpoint pattern (thread_id isolation, resume from checkpoint).
How to apply: Always pass only changed fields in non-UPS invocations. UserPromptSubmit is the only full state initializer (_fresh_state). When adding new hook logic that needs prior-turn data, read from state (it's in the checkpoint).

nodes.name: launchd-plist-reload-required
nodes.tags: launchd plist kickstart bootout bootstrap reload uvicorn deploy server restart
nodes.body:
`launchctl kickstart -k` restarts the process but does NOT re-read the plist from disk — launchd uses its in-memory loaded config. If the plist file was edited (e.g. removing --reload), the change is invisible until the service is fully unloaded and reloaded.

**Why:** Discovered when --reload persisted in the running process after plist was fixed on disk.

**How to apply:** After editing a plist file, always do `launchctl bootout gui/$(id -u)/<label> && launchctl bootstrap gui/$(id -u) ~/Library/LaunchAgents/<label>.plist` to apply the new config. deploy.sh's `launchctl kickstart` is only correct when the plist hasn't changed.

nodes.name: logs-read-tool
nodes.tags: logs,observability,hook-logs,claude-hooks
nodes.body: Always use the logs__read MCP tool to read hook logs (domain classification, memory retrieval, tool hints, session injection). All hook events write to claude_hooks.sqlite in iCloud via sqlite_log_handler.py. Never query the DB directly with sqlite3.

nodes.name: memory-add-batch-prefer-over-serial
nodes.tags: memory__add_batch, memory__add, batch upsert, writing memories, multiple memories, save memories
nodes.body:
When adding or updating multiple memories in one operation, use `mcp__claude-hooks__memory__add_batch` instead of N serial `memory__add` calls.

**Why:** `memory__add_batch` upserts all entries in a single DB transaction and a single vector embedding call — faster, fewer round-trips, and lets the user see all changes at once before confirming.

**How to apply:**
- Any time you need to write 2+ memories (domain fixes, bulk tagging, post-task lessons) → use `memory__add_batch(memories=[...])`
- Each entry: `{"name": "slug", "type": "...", "body": "...", "domain": "...", "tags": "..."}`
- Returns `{ok, count, results: [{name, action}]}` — partial failures reported per-entry, batch continues
- Single memory with no siblings → `memory__add` is fine

nodes.name: memory-add-batch-preserve-body
nodes.tags: memory add_batch upsert body placeholder overwrite recovery loss data JSONL
nodes.body:
Never use a placeholder value (e.g. 'existing') for body in memory__add_batch when doing a field-only update. The upsert overwrites the body column — all 92 memories lost their bodies this way.

**Why:** ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET body=excluded.body replaces the real content with the placeholder.
**How to apply:** Before a field-only update (e.g. adding `related` or `files`), fetch each memory's current body via memory__get and pass it through unchanged. Or use a direct SQL UPDATE targeting only the new column.

nodes.name: memory-atomicity-audit-pattern
nodes.tags: memory atomicity audit compound split duplicate staleness review one-fact one-file
nodes.body:
When auditing memories for atomicity, also check for near-duplicate memories covering the same topic — they accumulate over time without being flagged as "compound" but cause injection noise. Delete the weaker one and merge unique content into the stronger one.

Also check tags for stale references (e.g., client.sh renamed to client.py) — these silently degrade BM25 retrieval without any schema violation.

**Why:** The compound-memory audit caught one staleness fix and one duplicate merge that the original checklist didn't anticipate.

**How to apply:** During any memory audit pass, run three checks: (1) compound body, (2) near-duplicate name/tags across memories, (3) stale references in tags/body that no longer match current file/tool names.

nodes.name: memory-search-slug-normalization
nodes.tags: memory,search,slug,normalization,ux,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Memory search should normalize slugs before matching — strip underscores and hyphens from both the query and the slug, then compare.

**Why:** Users type slugs with underscores (claude_hooks_goals) but slugs are stored with hyphens (claude-hooks-goals), causing missed matches that waste a tool call.

**How to apply:** In `handle_search`, normalize the query and name field by replacing `_` and `-` with empty string (or a space) before LIKE comparison. Apply to the `name` column search path specifically.

nodes.name: mock-cfg-must-patch-retriever-config
nodes.tags: mock_cfg patch fixture langchain config retriever test isolation CombinationSignalRetriever
nodes.body:
When a retriever imports config inside its method body (`from langchain_learning.config import config as _cfg` inside retrieve()), patching the node module's `_cfg` is not enough — the retriever re-binds `_cfg` on every call. The fixture must also patch `langchain_learning.config.config` directly.

**Why:** `patch.object(lm, '_cfg', cfg)` only patches the load_memories module's reference. CombinationSignalRetriever imports fresh from langchain_learning.config on each call, bypassing that patch.

**How to apply:** Any test fixture that redirects the memory or tool DB path must patch both the node module AND `langchain_learning.config.config` (via `patch.object(lc, 'config', cfg)`) so that late-binding imports inside retrievers see the temp DB.

nodes.name: prefer-commit-rag-over-git
nodes.tags: diff_rag, commit-rag, git, search, workflow,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
Always use diff_rag__smart_search before falling back to git log/show/grep when looking for code patterns, prior implementations, or commit history.

**Why:** diff_rag is semantically indexed over all commits — it finds relevant hunks by meaning, not just keywords. git commands are slower and require knowing exact strings or commit refs.

**How to apply:** Any time the task involves "find how X was done before", "look for prior implementation of Y", or "check commit history for Z" — run diff_rag__smart_search first. Only use git log/show if the RAG search returns no useful results.

nodes.name: protocol-as-pipe-joint
nodes.tags: protocol interface pipe joint memory retrieval tool scoring gate policy pluggable substitution LCEL callable classes structural
nodes.body:
Protocols in claude-hooks mark the joints in the memory injection pipe where behavioral substitution is needed:
  prompt → tokenise → MemoryRetriever → LoadMemoriesNode → additionalSystemPrompt

The three protocol joints are:
  1. MemoryRetriever — how memories are scored and fetched (langchain_learning/nodes/load_memories.py)
  2. ToolScorer     — how tool hints are ranked (langchain_learning/nodes/score_tools.py)
  3. GatePolicy     — how tool gates are dispatched (hooks/gate_check.py)

Not every node gets a Protocol — only nodes that are genuine substitution points (where a different implementation could exist and be useful).

**Why:** Mirrors LCEL Runnable | Runnable composition but in pure Python with no framework overhead. Same idea as ACME POC NodeCallable Protocol (Principle 13).

**How to apply:** When adding a new node, ask: is this a substitution point? If no, skip the Protocol. If yes, add it to retrievers.py and keep the implementation in nodes/ or hooks/.

nodes.name: protocol-extraction-reduces-node-code
nodes.tags: protocol extraction refactor node code reduction callable class scoring retrieval clean separation
nodes.body:
Extracting inline node logic into a Protocol-backed class consistently reduces node code by 40-60%. The node's __call__ shrinks to: extract state fields → call the Protocol method → wrap in error handling → return partial state. All SQLite, config, and scoring logic migrates to the implementation class.

**Why:** The Protocol boundary forces the question 'what is this node's job?' — the answer is always 'transform state', not 'manage DB connections'. Implementation details naturally move out.

**How to apply:** When a node has inline DB access, scoring loops, or config reads, ask if those belong in a Protocol-backed class. If yes, extract. The node gets simpler; the scorer/retriever gets testable in isolation.

nodes.name: replay-baseline-recapture-after-memory-add
nodes.tags: replay harness baseline recapture memories drift deviation test failure
nodes.body:
Replay harness deviations can be caused by adding new memories to MEMORY.sqlite during a session — new memories change scores and surface in related_tasks, causing baseline mismatches. Not always a code regression.

**Why:** The baseline is a snapshot of what the graph returns for a fixed input. New memories in the live DB change those outputs legitimately.

**How to apply:** After any session that adds multiple memories, recapture: `uv run python tests/replay_harness.py --capture`. The baseline is gitignored — it lives locally only. If deviations appear after memory additions (not after code changes), recapture is the fix, not a bug investigation.

nodes.name: replay-session-ui-filter
nodes.tags: replay harness session_id prefix filter ui task detail task_events display noise
nodes.body:
Replay harness fires real prompts with `session_id` prefixed `"replay-"`. These log T1 events to `task_events` against the active task, appearing as noise in the /ui/tasks/ detail panel.

Fix (hooks/server.py `ui_task_detail`): filter at display time:
```python
turns = [e for e in history if e.get("tools") != "decision" and not e.get("session_id", "").startswith("replay-")]
```

**Why:** Fix at display time not write time — replay events in task_events remain useful for test diagnostics; only the UI view is filtered.

**Historical cleanup:** 1025 replay rows were deleted from proj_tasks.db task_events in June 2026 — future replay rows remain in DB but are hidden in UI.

nodes.name: retriever-top-n-none-not-hardcoded
nodes.tags: top_n retriever protocol signature default config scoring memory replay harness
nodes.body:
Protocol method signatures that have config-driven defaults must use `None` as the default, not a hardcoded int. Example: `retrieve(tokens, project_domain, top_n: int | None = None)` — then the implementation reads from `load_scoring_cfg()` when `top_n is None`. Hardcoding `top_n=5` silently overrode `memory_top_n=10` from src.config, causing 6 replay harness deviations.

**Why:** Replay harness caught this immediately — baseline had memories_count=10, replayed got 5. Any config-tunable limit belongs in the implementation, not the Protocol default.

**How to apply:** When extracting an inline scoring/retrieval function into a Protocol-backed class, check all numeric defaults against what the original code read from config. If it used config, the new default should be None.

nodes.name: retrievers-lazy-import-pattern
nodes.tags: lazy import circular import retrievers protocol implementation method body load-time dependency
nodes.body:
In retrievers.py, implementation classes (CombinationSignalRetriever, KeywordOverlapScorer, DefaultGatePolicy) do all their imports inside the method body, not at module top. This is the circular-import escape hatch — retrievers.py must be importable without loading nodes/ or hooks/.

**Why:** langchain_learning.config is imported by nodes/ which also imports from retrievers.py. A module-level `from langchain_learning.config import config` in retrievers.py would create a load-time circular dependency.

**How to apply:** Any new implementation class added to retrievers.py must follow the same rule — all non-stdlib, non-typing imports go inside the method body. Protocols themselves can stay at module top (they only use typing constructs).

nodes.name: retrievers-py-import-safety-rule
nodes.tags: protocol import safety dependency arrow circular import retrievers langchain nodes hooks
nodes.body:
When a Protocol definition file must be import-safe, the only tolerable external import is a *type* used in a method signature. retrievers.py imports GateContext from hooks/gates.py purely for the GatePolicy.check() signature — it never calls into hooks/. The test: if you delete hooks/, does retrievers.py still compile? If yes, the dependency arrow is correct.

**Why:** nodes/ and hooks/ both import from retrievers.py. If retrievers.py imported back from either, circular imports would break at load time.

**How to apply:** Any new Protocol file in this codebase must follow the same rule — zero callable imports from the modules that will import it. Type-only imports (used only in signatures) are acceptable.

nodes.name: server-memory-no-assistant-turns
nodes.tags: server-memory, event-log, assistant-turns, logging
nodes.body:
The server memory event log deliberately does NOT store assistant turns — only `prompt` (user messages) and `tool` (tool calls) events are captured.

**Why:** Removed by design — assistant turns were explicitly excluded from the event store.

**How to apply:** Do not suggest adding assistant turn logging to the server memory system.

nodes.name: server-project-root-hardcoded
nodes.tags: server.py project root worktree static files templates css deploy bug paths.py
nodes.body:
hooks/server.py previously had `_PROJECT_ROOT = Path.home() / 'workspace/claude-hooks'` hardcoded. This caused StaticFiles and templates to always serve from the main worktree, so CSS/template changes deployed to claude-hooks-test were invisible — the server silently served stale files from main.

**Fix applied:** All paths now live in `hooks/paths.py`. Import with `from hooks.paths import PROJECT_ROOT as _PROJECT_ROOT`. `PROJECT_ROOT` resolves via `Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent` — worktree-safe by design.

**Why:** Path.home()-based hardcode doesn't account for the git worktree model (dev/test/main are separate dirs).

**How to apply:** Never hardcode paths in server.py or any module that mounts static files. Always import from `hooks/paths.py`. Verify after deploy with `curl /ui/static/ui.css | grep <changed-rule>`.

nodes.name: session-id-how-to
nodes.tags: session_id,session,mcp,Turn state,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
session_id is always available in the ## Turn state system prompt block (injected by the hook). Never guess it.

To look up sessions via MCP:
- Use mcp__claude-hooks__hooks__checkpoint_query to read the current session state including session_id, prompt_id, domains, keywords.
- Use curl http://127.0.0.1:8766/session for a quick session list from the live server.

NOTE: mcp__local-mac__session__* tools (session__list_ids, session__get, session__current) have been deleted — they read langgraph_checkpoints.db which is owned exclusively by claude-hooks. Use checkpoint_query instead.

**Why:** session__list returned oversized payloads that caused tool result truncation. session__* tools in claude_for_mac_local were a boundary violation — removed 2026-06-23.
**How to apply:** Always read session_id from ## Turn state. Use hooks__checkpoint_query for programmatic access to checkpoint state.

nodes.name: small-task-use-existing-parent
nodes.tags: task, parent, epic, small task, one-step, task creation, task framework, related tasks, hydration
nodes.body:
For small or one-step tasks, don't create a new task — log the work under an existing closely related epic or task as parent.

**Why:** Avoids task sprawl AND hydrates the related-task graph over time. Small tasks logged under the right epic build a trail of what work has touched that area — `load_related_tasks` picks this up and gives Claude richer context in future sessions. The ancestry chain becomes the epic's memory of how it evolved.

**How to apply:**
- If the work is a single commit or a single clear action → find the most relevant open epic and use it as parent
- Use `/task-framework` or `tasks__list` to scan open epics before creating anything new
- Only create a standalone task when the work has multiple steps, a non-obvious design decision, or needs its own tracking trail

nodes.name: sqlite-idempotent-column-migration
nodes.tags: sqlite alter table migration idempotent column add schema try except OperationalError
nodes.body:
SQLite ALTER TABLE has no IF NOT EXISTS — adding a column that already exists raises OperationalError. Wrap each ADD COLUMN statement in try/except OperationalError for idempotent migrations.

Pattern used in src/tools/memory.py:
```python
_NEW_COLUMNS = [
    "ALTER TABLE memories ADD COLUMN last_validated TIMESTAMP",
    "ALTER TABLE memories ADD COLUMN files TEXT",
    "ALTER TABLE memories ADD COLUMN docs TEXT",
]

def _ensure_schema(con):
    for stmt in _NEW_COLUMNS:
        try:
            con.execute(stmt)
        except sqlite3.OperationalError:
            pass  # column already exists
```

Called at the top of each DB connection block in handle_add and handle_add_batch.

**Why:** SQLite doesn't support IF NOT EXISTS on ALTER TABLE (unlike CREATE TABLE). This pattern is safe to call on every connect — no-op on existing columns, applies on fresh DBs.

**How to apply:** Any time a new column is needed in an existing table, add it to _NEW_COLUMNS and call _ensure_schema(con) at the start of each connection block that writes to that table.

nodes.name: task-always-add-completion-memory
nodes.tags: task completion memory introspection retrospective lesson learned always add
nodes.body:
Always add at least one memory at the end of every task, drawn from the distilled lesson in the task body's 'On completion' note. If nothing architecture-worthy emerged, add a process or feedback memory instead. Never skip silently.

**Why:** The memory system is the introspection framework — task completions are the primary feed. Each task should leave a trace that helps future tasks, even if it's just a negative result ('this approach was tried and discarded because X').

**How to apply:** Before calling tasks__finish, ask: what would help a future session starting this work cold? Write that as a memory. Use memory__add_batch so it's one call.

nodes.name: task-body-checklist-for-steps
nodes.tags: task, create, checklist, resolution, body, steps, refactor, removal, task-create
nodes.body:
For removal, refactor, or any task with a known list of discrete steps, write the steps as a markdown checklist inside the Resolution: section — not numbered prose.

**Why:** Prose steps are write-once. A checklist can be ticked off with tasks__update(body=...) as each step completes, making the task body a live progress tracker rather than a static plan. The body gate only checks that sections exist — it doesn't constrain content inside them, so checklists are always valid.

**How to apply:** When calling tasks__create or tasks__update for a refactor/removal/migration task, format Resolution: like:
```
Resolution:
- [ ] src/tools/tasks.py — remove _ISSUE_TYPES review entry
- [ ] hooks/gates.py — remove _REVIEW_TAG_RE
- [ ] delete load_active_review.py
```
Tick items with `- [x]` via tasks__update as each is done. Also applies when using /task-create — default to checklist format in Resolution whenever the task has 3+ discrete file or step targets.

nodes.name: task-framework-memory-delegation
nodes.tags: task-framework, memory injection, delegation, skill, targeted fetch, memory__search, scale
nodes.body:
Planned: delegate targeted memory fetching to the /task-framework skill itself as memory corpus grows.

**Why:** BM25 across 150+ memories loses precision — tag overlap becomes noisy at scale. The skill has richer context (active task, domain, phase) to make a targeted pull at the moment work begins, rather than relying on general UPS-time scoring.

**How to apply:**
- UPS hook remains a fast general-context pass (session orientation, top-N BM25)
- /task-framework skill adds a `memory__search` or `memory__list` call scoped to domain + task type at task activation time
- Two-tier injection: hook = broad, skill = precise

**When:** revisit when claude-hooks domain crosses ~150 memories.

nodes.name: task-title-keyword-quality
nodes.tags: task title, title quality, keywords, semantic search, related tasks, embedding, load_related_tasks, neighbor, what where why
nodes.body:
Dev task titles must encode what + where + why with concrete keywords — not activity descriptions.
Good: "Add memories column to task_events for per-turn injection logging"
Bad: "fix gate", "run tests", "log memory ids"

**Why:** The title is embedded by load_related_tasks for semantic neighbor search. Vague titles don't surface as neighbors for related work, breaking the feedback loop that makes related-task context useful.

**How to apply:** Before calling tasks__create, check the title contains the module/file name, the specific thing being changed, and the reason. Reject titles that are pure activities.

nodes.name: tasks-active-status-checkpoint-only
nodes.tags: tasks status active checkpoint open_tasks DB valid_statuses transitions architecture
nodes.body:
'active' is NOT a valid DB status in open_tasks. Task activation lives in the LangGraph checkpoint (active_task_id) only — tasks__set_active never writes to the DB. open_tasks.status values are: open, done, abandoned, blocked.

**Why:** Multiple sessions can each have a different active task; the DB has no concept of per-session active state. Checkpoint is the right owner.

**How to apply:** Never set status='active' when inserting/updating open_tasks. Never filter by status='active' in DB queries. Use _get_active_session() + checkpoint scan for live-session display, but gate on DB status for closed tasks.

nodes.name: tasks-migrate-check-correct-table
nodes.tags: tasks migrate PRAGMA table_info open_tasks task_events ALTER TABLE column migration bug
nodes.body:
When adding a migration guard in _migrate(), always query PRAGMA table_info for the table you intend to ALTER. The original _migrate() only kept one `cols` set for task_events — adding a guard for open_tasks using that same set caused 'duplicate column name' errors because the check was against the wrong table.

**Why:** Was burned when adding the keywords column — checked `task_events` cols but applied ALTER to `open_tasks`, causing all unit tests to fail.

**How to apply:** Use separate variables — `event_cols` for task_events, `task_cols` for open_tasks — any time you add a new guard to _migrate().

nodes.name: tasks-ui-live-session-gate
nodes.tags: tasks ui live session checkpoint scan done abandoned status authoritative server.py ui_task_detail
nodes.body:
ui_task_detail scans all LangGraph checkpoints to find which session has a task active (for the live-turn badge). This scan must be gated: skip it when DB status is 'done' or 'abandoned'.

**Why:** Checkpoint scan is cross-session and can show stale 'active' badge after tasks__finish if another session had it loaded. DB status is authoritative for closed tasks.

**How to apply:** `if task.get('status') not in ('done', 'abandoned'):` before the checkpointer.list() loop in ui_task_detail.

nodes.name: tool-hints-saturation-problem
nodes.tags: tool-hints,bm25,retrieval,scoring,saturation,universal-tools,claude-hooks
nodes.body:
High-frequency universal tools (vault__write, vault__read, session__save_summary) accumulate counts fast and will eventually dominate BM25+domain retrieval, crowding out lower-count but contextually precise tools.

**Why:** Count-weighted scoring has no ceiling — a tool used 500x scores 10x higher than one used 50x, even if the 50x tool is the right one for the prompt.

**Plan needed:** Implement a saturation cap or IDF-style normalization in ToolHintsRetriever. Options:
1. Clamp count contribution above threshold N (e.g. log(count) instead of count for ranking)
2. Flag tools as universal (domain=global + count > threshold) and exclude from scored retrieval — always inject them separately as ambient context
3. Two-tier retrieval: universal tools in a fixed header, BM25 scores only non-universal tools

**How to apply:** Revisit when any global-domain tool exceeds ~100 uses or when retrieval quality noticeably degrades (wrong tools surfacing for niche prompts).
